abstract: A central challenge in the emerging field of molecular spintronics lies in identifying new spin qubit platforms capable of meeting the rigorous demands of quantum information science. Recent studies suggest that photoexcited chromophore-radical systems are particularly promising candidates for expanding this chemical repertoire. In this work, we employ transient electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR) spectroscopy to investigate a series of three molecular architectures featuring a perylene diimide (PDI) chromophore covalently linked to a stable 2,2,6,6-tetramethylpiperidin-1-oxyl (TEMPO) radical. By systematically exploring the effects of isotope labeling within the TEMPO moiety, we characterize the EPR signatures and electron spin coherence of the photo-generated molecular quartet states. Our results demonstrate that the spin states of these systems can be coherently manipulated even at room temperature, achieving a spin coherence time ($T_2$) of 0.7 μs. This observation of robust electron spin coherence under ambient conditions represents a significant step toward the integration of molecular qubits into functional, real-world quantum devices.
